content.text: assigns a probability to a sequence of tokens. Its core task is to estimate how likely the next token is, given all the tokens that came before it:

attrs.latex: P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_T) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(w_t \mid w_1, \ldots, w_{t-1})

content.text: — says that the joint probability of a sequence equals the product of conditional probabilities of each token given its history. A model that learns these conditionals well can
text by sampling one token at a time.
Modern language models are trained with

. Given a document of T tokens, the model sees the first t-1 tokens and must predict token t — for every t in the document simultaneously. The loss is the average negative log-likelihood across all positions:

This is cross-entropy loss between the model's predicted distribution over the vocabulary and the one-hot true token. Minimising it forces the model to assign high probability to the actual next token.

attrs.alt: Diagram showing next-token prediction: each prefix of 'The cat sat on the mat' becomes a training pair with the following token as the target

content.text: Because future tokens must not influence the prediction of the current token, transformer language models apply a

: position t can only attend to positions 1 through t. This makes training efficient — one forward pass over a sequence of T tokens produces T-1 training signal pairs — while ensuring the model learns a valid autoregressive distribution.

import torch

def causal_mask(seq_len: int) -> torch.Tensor:
    """Returns upper-triangular mask; 0 = attend, -inf = block."""
    mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(seq_len, seq_len), diagonal=1)
    return mask.masked_fill(mask == 1, float('-inf'))

print(causal_mask(4))
# tensor([[  0., -inf, -inf, -inf],
#         [  0.,   0., -inf, -inf],
#         [  0.,   0.,   0., -inf],
#         [  0.,   0.,   0.,   0.]])
# Row i can attend to columns 0..i only.
Three Variants of the LM Objective
Causal LM is the dominant objective for modern large language models because the autoregressive property makes generation straightforward: sample a token, append it to context, repeat.

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

# Vocabulary size V=5, sequence length T=4
V, T = 5, 4

# Model output: logits for each position (T, V)
logits = torch.tensor([
    [2.0, 0.5, 0.1, 0.3, 0.1],  # position 1 predicts position 2
    [0.1, 3.0, 0.2, 0.1, 0.1],  # position 2 predicts position 3
    [0.2, 0.1, 0.1, 2.5, 0.1],  # position 3 predicts position 4
    [0.1, 0.1, 0.1, 0.1, 3.0],  # position 4 predicts position 5 (if it existed)
])

# True next-token ids (the targets — shifted by one from inputs)
targets = torch.tensor([1, 2, 3, 4])  # token ids

# Cross-entropy averages over positions automatically
loss = F.cross_entropy(logits, targets)
print(f"Loss: {loss.item():.4f}")  # lower → model is more confident about correct tokens

# In practice: logits come from model(input_ids), targets = input_ids[:, 1:]
Once trained, the model converts logits to a probability distribution over the vocabulary with softmax and samples the next token. The choice of
controls the quality and diversity of the output:
— always pick the highest-probability token. Fast, deterministic, tends to be repetitive.
— divide logits by temperature T before softmax. T < 1 sharpens the distribution (more deterministic); T > 1 flattens it (more random).
— zero out all but the k most probable tokens, then sample. Prevents very low-probability tokens from ever being chosen.
— keep the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds p. Adapts the candidate pool to the sharpness of the distribution.
— maintain B candidate sequences in parallel, keeping only the B highest-probability paths at each step. Better for tasks with a single correct answer (translation, code).

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM
import torch

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2")
model.eval()

prompt = "The language model objective is"
input_ids = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt").input_ids

with torch.no_grad():
    # Greedy decoding
    greedy = model.generate(input_ids, max_new_tokens=20)
    # Sampling: temperature=0.9, top-p=0.95
    sampled = model.generate(
        input_ids,
        do_sample=True,
        temperature=0.9,
        top_p=0.95,
        max_new_tokens=20,
    )

print(tokenizer.decode(greedy[0], skip_special_tokens=True))
print(tokenizer.decode(sampled[0], skip_special_tokens=True))

Next-token prediction is a deceptively general objective. To predict the next word in a sentence about medicine, the model must implicitly learn medicine. To predict the next line in a code file, it must learn syntax and semantics. A sufficiently large model trained on a sufficiently large corpus develops broad capabilities as a side-effect of excelling at this one task — a property that explains the emergence of reasoning and instruction-following in large language models.

The language model objective is the engine that powers every large language model in production today — GPT-4, Claude, Gemini, LLaMA, Mistral — yet it is deceptively simple: predict the next token. Before this objective was applied at scale to Transformers, training NLP models required carefully annotated datasets for each specific task: one labeled corpus for sentiment analysis, another for named entity recognition, another for machine translation. Gathering these datasets was expensive, time-consuming, and produced models that could not transfer knowledge across tasks.

The LM objective solved this bottleneck by using raw text as its own label. Every document on the internet is implicitly a supervised training example: the context is the label for the next word, and the next word is the label for the context. This insight, combined with massive compute and data, created models that exhibit emergent capabilities — code generation, multi-step reasoning, translation — as side effects of simply becoming very good at predicting the next token.

The practical pain point it addresses is annotation cost. Building a question-answering system previously required thousands of human-annotated (question, answer) pairs. Today, a language model fine-tuned from a base model pre-trained with the LM objective can answer questions with only dozens or hundreds of examples — and with Instruction Tuning and RLHF on top, sometimes zero labeled QA pairs at all.
Any text corpus is a training set. Common Crawl alone provides trillions of tokens with no labeling cost, enabling training at scales impossible with human annotation.

A single base model pre-trained with the LM objective can be fine-tuned for classification, summarisation, translation, code generation, and structured extraction — eliminating the need to train task-specific architectures from scratch.
Captures syntax, semantics, and world knowledge jointly.
To predict the next word in a medical paper, the model must implicitly learn anatomy, drug names, and clinical reasoning. Knowledge is compressed into weights as a side effect of the objective.
A 2048-token document produces 2047 training pairs in a single forward pass, making causal LM training extremely sample-efficient relative to per-document-level supervision.
Unlike discriminative objectives (classification, regression), the LM objective trains the model to generate — making text production a first-class capability rather than a post-hoc adapter.

The LM objective optimises for next-token prediction accuracy, not for truthfulness, safety, or usefulness to a human user. A model trained purely on next-token prediction will reproduce harmful content, perpetuate biases in its training corpus, and hallucinate facts — because all of these outputs can have high likelihood under the training distribution. The model is incentivised to sound plausible, not to be accurate.

Perplexity (the exponentiated average negative log-likelihood) is the primary training metric, but it is a poor proxy for downstream utility. A model can achieve low perplexity by memorising rare but highly predictable patterns (boilerplate legal text, repetitive code) while still struggling with novel compositional reasoning. This explains why perplexity and human preference scores (e.g., from RLHF reward models) are not tightly correlated: you need both metrics to characterise model quality.

The LM objective also treats all tokens equally — a mundane word like "the" counts identically to a crucial factual claim. This means errors on high-stakes tokens (names, numbers, code operators) are underweighted in the loss unless the data distribution is engineered to oversample them.
When to Use the LM Objective (and When Not To)

Use the causal LM objective when you need a generative model — one that produces free-form text, continues a prompt, or is the foundation for fine-tuning towards instruction following or dialogue. Use Masked LM (BERT-style) when your task is purely discriminative: classification, sequence labelling, extractive question answering, or embedding retrieval. Use encoder-decoder objectives (T5's span corruption) when your task is structured text transformation: translation, summarisation with a fixed-length constraint, or structured extraction into a schema.

Perplexity is the standard metric for evaluating how well a language model predicts a held-out text. It is the exponentiated average negative log-likelihood — intuitively, the "average number of equally likely choices" the model faces at each token:

A perplexity of 10 means the model behaves as if it is uniformly choosing between 10 equally probable tokens at each position. A perplexity of 1 means it perfectly predicts every token. Real language models achieve perplexity of 5–20 on standard English benchmarks (Penn Treebank, WikiText-103), with lower values indicating better language modelling.

import torch
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM
import math

def compute_perplexity(text: str, model_name: str = "gpt2") -> float:
    """
    Compute perplexity of a text string under a causal language model.
    Uses sliding window to handle sequences longer than context length.
    """
    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
    model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_name)
    model.eval()

    encodings = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt")
    max_length = model.config.max_position_embeddings
    stride = 512  # sliding window stride
    input_ids = encodings.input_ids
    seq_len = input_ids.size(1)

    nlls = []
    prev_end_loc = 0
    for begin_loc in range(0, seq_len, stride):
        end_loc   = min(begin_loc + max_length, seq_len)
        trg_len   = end_loc - prev_end_loc
        chunk_ids = input_ids[:, begin_loc:end_loc]
        target_ids = chunk_ids.clone()
        target_ids[:, :-trg_len] = -100  # mask non-target prefix

        with torch.no_grad():
            outputs = model(chunk_ids, labels=target_ids)
            neg_log_likelihood = outputs.loss * trg_len

        nlls.append(neg_log_likelihood)
        prev_end_loc = end_loc
        if end_loc == seq_len:
            break

    ppl = torch.exp(torch.stack(nlls).sum() / seq_len)
    return ppl.item()

sample_text = (
    "The Transformer architecture relies on self-attention mechanisms "
    "that allow each token to attend to all other tokens in the sequence."
)
ppl = compute_perplexity(sample_text)
print(f"Perplexity: {ppl:.2f}")
Real-World Example: Fine-Tuning a Code LM

A software company wants to build an internal code completion model for their proprietary codebase. They start from a public LLM (e.g., CodeLlama-7B) pre-trained with the causal LM objective on open-source code, then continue pre-training on their private code corpus, and finally instruction-tune it for function docstring generation. Each stage uses the same LM objective.

from transformers import (
    AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, TrainingArguments,
    DataCollatorForLanguageModeling, Trainer
)
from datasets import load_dataset
import torch

# ── Stage 1: Continued pre-training on private code corpus ────────────────
model_name = "codellama/CodeLlama-7b-hf"
tokenizer  = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
model      = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    model_name, torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16
)

# Tokenise code files
def tokenize(examples):
    return tokenizer(
        examples["code"],
        truncation=True,
        max_length=2048,
        return_overflowing_tokens=True,  # pack full 2048-token windows
        return_length=True,
    )

raw_dataset = load_dataset("json", data_files="private_code.jsonl")
tokenized   = raw_dataset.map(tokenize, batched=True, remove_columns=["code"])

# DataCollator shifts labels right by 1 automatically (CLM objective)
collator = DataCollatorForLanguageModeling(tokenizer=tokenizer, mlm=False)

training_args = TrainingArguments(
    output_dir="code-lm-continued",
    per_device_train_batch_size=4,
    gradient_accumulation_steps=8,  # effective batch size = 32
    num_train_epochs=3,
    learning_rate=2e-5,
    lr_scheduler_type="cosine",
    warmup_ratio=0.05,
    bf16=True,
    logging_steps=50,
    save_steps=500,
    evaluation_strategy="steps",
    eval_steps=500,
    load_best_model_at_end=True,
    metric_for_best_model="eval_loss",  # minimise cross-entropy loss
)

trainer = Trainer(
    model=model,
    args=training_args,
    train_dataset=tokenized["train"],
    eval_dataset=tokenized["validation"],
    data_collator=collator,
)
trainer.train()

# Perplexity from eval loss: PPL = exp(eval_loss)
import math
eval_results = trainer.evaluate()
ppl = math.exp(eval_results["eval_loss"])
print(f"Validation perplexity: {ppl:.2f}")
expects them to be the same as input_ids (shifted internally). For
, labels must mark only the [MASK] positions (-100 elsewhere). Mixing these up silently trains on wrong targets.
Not masking the prompt in instruction tuning.

During supervised fine-tuning (SFT), you want the model to learn to generate the response, not memorise the instruction. Set labels = -100 for instruction tokens; compute loss only on response tokens.
Using perplexity as the sole evaluation metric.

Perplexity measures average token prediction quality but does not capture coherence, factual accuracy, or helpfulness. Always supplement with task-specific benchmarks (HumanEval for code, MMLU for knowledge, MT-bench for instruction following).
Padding short sequences to max_length wastes GPU compute and inflates effective batch size with padding tokens. Use
to pack multiple documents into a single 2048-token window.
Contaminating test data in the training corpus.
If benchmark questions appear verbatim in pre-training data, benchmark scores are inflated. Deduplicate aggressively and withhold evaluation data from the training corpus.
Start with a linear warmup (5% of steps), then cosine decay to 10% of peak LR. Abrupt LR drops cause loss spikes; cosine schedules are empirically more stable for LM pre-training.
Clip gradients to 1.0 and log the pre-clipping norm per step. Spikes above 5× the running average often indicate corrupt training examples or tokenisation bugs that should be investigated.
Separate data domains by sampling rate.

Mix data sources (code, web, books, papers) with explicit sample weights rather than concatenating. Upsampling high-quality sources (books, curated code) relative to raw web text consistently improves downstream performance.
Track both train and validation loss.

The gap between them indicates overfitting. With large pre-training corpora (>100B tokens), overfitting is rare for models under 7B parameters, but for continued pre-training on small domain corpora it is a real risk.

LM pre-training compute is governed by the Chinchilla scaling law: for a given compute budget C (FLOPs), the optimal model size N and number of training tokens D satisfy N ≈ D and the optimal token count is roughly 20× the model parameter count. A 7B-parameter model should train on ~140B tokens for compute-optimal pre-training. Violating this by training a large model on too few tokens (as GPT-3 did) leaves performance gains on the table.

attrs.latex: C \approx 6 \cdot N \cdot D \quad \text{(FLOPs for one training run)}

content.text: In practice, throughput is the binding constraint. With tensor parallelism across 8 H100 GPUs (SXM), a 7B-parameter model processes roughly 80K–100K tokens per second. Training on 140B tokens then requires about 1.5 million GPU-seconds, or roughly 48 GPU-hours on an 8-GPU node. Pre-training a 70B model on 1.4T tokens takes ~100× longer — on the order of 4,000 GPU-hours on 64 H100s.

The standard toolkit for LM training has converged around a small set of libraries. Hugging Face Transformers provides model architectures and training utilities. The Trainer API wraps the training loop for fine-tuning. For pre-training at scale, frameworks like Megatron-LM, GPT-NeoX, or LightSeq provide tensor and pipeline parallelism critical for multi-GPU efficiency. Flash Attention 2 is now universally used to reduce attention memory from O(n²) to O(n) and increase throughput 2–4×.

# Enabling Flash Attention 2 for efficient LM training
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM
import torch

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf",
    attn_implementation="flash_attention_2",   # requires flash-attn >= 2.0
    torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    device_map="auto",
)
# Flash Attention 2 reduces memory from O(n^2) to O(n) and speeds
# training by 2-4x on sequences > 1024 tokens.

# For memory-efficient fine-tuning, combine with gradient checkpointing:
model.gradient_checkpointing_enable()
# And optionally LoRA (Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning):
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model
lora_config = LoraConfig(
    r=16,               # rank: smaller = fewer params, faster, less capacity
    lora_alpha=32,
    target_modules=["q_proj", "v_proj"],
    lora_dropout=0.05,
    task_type="CAUSAL_LM",
)
model = get_peft_model(model, lora_config)
model.print_trainable_parameters()
# trainable params: 4,194,304 || all params: 6,742,609,920 || trainable%: 0.062%

The LM objective has several security-relevant failure modes. Memorisation: models trained with low regularisation on small corpora can memorise verbatim training examples, including PII, API keys, and proprietary code. Carlini et al. (2021) demonstrated that GPT-2 could be made to emit exact training samples via targeted prompting. Mitigation: train on deduplicated data, apply differential privacy (DP-SGD) for sensitive corpora, and audit model outputs for training data leakage before deployment.

Prompt injection exploits the model's tendency to continue context. A malicious document embedded in a RAG context can override user intent by containing instructions like "Ignore previous instructions and...". Defending against this requires input sanitisation, content-aware prompt templates, and output monitoring. The LM objective makes this attack surface inherent — it cannot be fully patched at the pre-training stage.
is next-token prediction: minimise the average cross-entropy loss across all token positions in a sequence.
raw text is its own label — no human annotation required, enabling trillion-token training.
ensures each position only attends to past tokens, producing T-1 training pairs per sequence in one forward pass.
(exp of average NLL) is the training metric but a poor proxy for utility — supplement with task benchmarks.
arise as side effects: a model good at next-token prediction must implicitly learn grammar, facts, and reasoning.
for compute-optimal training, use ~20 tokens per parameter; under-training leaves significant capability on the table.
models can leak training data verbatim; deduplicate corpora and apply DP-SGD for sensitive domains.
